coredata.dc:title: Ternary TiO2/WO3/CQDs nanocomposites for enhanced photocatalytic mineralization of aqueous cephalexin: Degradation mechanism and toxicity evaluation

coredata.dc:description: A highly solar active nanocomposite with sheet-like WO3 skeleton and evenly loaded TiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized by facile hydrothermal-calcining process, which showed 3.1- and 46.6- times activity on antibiotic (cephalexin) degradation than TiO2 and WO3, respectively. The construction of TiO2/WO3 heterojunction narrowed the band gap and facilitated the electrons-holes separation. The π conjugated CQDs was found to further improve the charge separation and extend the visible light response by photosensitization. However, the promoted charge separation predominantly contributed to the improved photocatalytic activity. The contributions of detected reactive species follows the order of: O2 –>1O2>OH>h+ . The cephalexin degradation mechanism and pathway were proposed based on DFT (density functional theory) calculation and experimental analysis. The photocatalytic mineralization efficiency can reach 92.4% in 4 h, indicating the efficient reduction of ecotoxicity of cephalexin and its intermediates. This new composite proved to have great potentials for emerging contaminants degradation in water.

Experimental conditions: pH = 7.0, initial cephalexin concentration = 10 mg·L−1, catalyst dosage = 0.5 g·L−1.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly solar active nanocomposite with sheet-like WO3 skeleton and evenly loaded TiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized by facile hydrothermal-calcining process, which showed 3.1- and 46.6- times activity on antibiotic (cephalexin) degradation than TiO2 and WO3, respectively. The construction of TiO2/WO3 heterojunction narrowed the band gap and facilitated the electrons-holes separation. The π conjugated CQDs was found to further improve the charge separation and extend the visible light response by photosensitization. However, the promoted charge separation predominantly contributed to the improved photocatalytic activity. The contributions of detected reactive species follows the order of: O2 –>1O2>OH>h+ . The cephalexin degradation mechanism and pathway were proposed based on DFT (density functional theory) calculation and experimental analysis. The photocatalytic mineralization efficiency can reach 92.4% in 4 h, indicating the efficient reduction of ecotoxicity of cephalexin and its intermediates. This new composite proved to have great potentials for emerging contaminants degradation in water.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been widely used to prevent and treat bacterial infections in humans and animals [1]. The continuous growth of global population and medical expenditure led to substantial increase of antibiotics consumption in the past few decades. For example, the global antibiotics production reached 248 000 tons in 2013, which had almost tripled since 2009, and the consumption of antibiotics was about 162 000 tons [2]. Antibiotics cannot be fully assimilated by humans and animals, thus the orally consumed antibiotics are finally excreted in the form of biotransformed metabolites or other by-products [3]. In addition, large amount of antibiotics are discharged from manufacturing processes, concentrated animal feeding operations, improperly disposal of expired pharmaceuticals [4]. Numerous studies have revealed that antibiotics are widely presented in wastewater, surface water, groundwater or even tap water with the concentration ranging from ng·L−1 to even mg·L−1 level [5]. Antibiotics can provide selective pressure on the natural bacteria and induce the formation of antibiotic resistance genes, thus reducing the therapeutic effectiveness against human and animal pathogens [6]. Therefore, the antibiotics in aquatic environment have raised significant concerns due to their potential threat to environment and human health in recent year [6].

The advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), i.e. ozonation, Fenton process, etc. are routinely applied to treat antibiotic wastewater, and always achieved satisfactory antibiotics decomposition efficiency [7]. However, these methods are frequently meeting the limitations of high equipment costs and energy consumption (i.e. for ozonation) or high chemical consumptions and solid wastes productions (i.e. for Fenton process) [8]. The photocatalytic oxidation has shown promising application potential for removing antibiotics due to its high degradation efficiency (>80%) [9,10] and mineralization level (40%–80% of total organic carbon removal) [11,12]. In recent years, the solar/visible light active photocatalysts have been developed, and showed high efficiency on utilizing the green solar energy to degrade antibiotics [9,10].

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) was widely considered as a potential photocatalyst due to its high UV activity, non-toxicity, high stability, and low cost [13]. However, TiO2 suffers from low visible light activity due to its wide band gap, which restricts its light utilization to the UV portion only. Moreover, the rapid electron-hole recombination rate is another inherent property restraining the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2 [14,15]. Therefore, various methods have been applied to promote the photocatalytic of TiO2. Constructing heterojunctions with visible light active semiconductors and incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with TiO2 have recently garnered vast attention [14,15].

The heterojunction of TiO2 with WO3 has been considered as a promising strategy because the obtain photocatalyst owns extended visible light adsorption and retarded electron-hole pairs recombination by the incorporation of WO3 [16]. Moreover, the loading of WO3 on TiO2 introduced surface acidity of the material, which could facilitate the adsorption of contaminants and promote the radical generation [17]. However, the materials are still confronting rapid charge carrier recombination. CQDs have attracted extensive attention in recent years due to their excellent up-converting photoluminescence performance [18], electron reservoir properties, and good photo-induced electron transfer [19]. The CQDs emissions cover a broad spectrum ranging from visible to near-infrared wavelength, which includes >50% of the solar spectrum. Therefore, the coupling of CQDs with other semiconductors emerged as a new way to improve the solar light spectrum utilization. The CQDs on the composites can act as electron reservoir to improve the photogenerated electron-hole pairs separation. Furthermore, the strong chemical interaction between the CQDs and other photocatalyst makes the material stable and further improved the photocatalytic performance of the material [20]. Until now, both constructing TiO2/WO3 heterojunction and coupling of CQDs with TiO2 have been well studied, which reveal that the coupling of TiO2/WO3 with CQDs may offer the material higher visible light utilization efficiency and promote charger carrier separation. However, this promising material synthesis strategy has not been studied yet, and the effectiveness and the key reaction mechanisms are unknown, especially the contributions of retarded electrons-holes recombination and extended visible light utilization on the promoted photocatalytic activity are not clear.

To this end, based on the hypothesis that both constructing the TiO2/WO3 heterojunction and incorporation of CQDs can enlarge the visible light absorbance and retard the electron-hole pairs recombination, we constructed a ternary heterojunction (TiO2/WO3/CQDs) by loading TiO2 and CQDs on the WO3 skeleton through hydrothermal-calcining method. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of the material and the underlying mechanism on the photocatalytic degradation of cephalexin (a model antibiotic) were comprehensively investigated and explained via material characterization, photocatalytic degradation experiments, density functional theory (DFT) calculation and radical analysis. Additionally, the degree of cephalexin mineralization and the ecotoxicity of the degradation intermediates were studied to assess the environmental risk of treated water, and the stability and recyclability of materials had also been evaluated for their application potentials.

Tungsten trioxide (WO3) with particle size of 50–60 nm was acquired from Jingcheng Chemical Co., Ltd (Wenzhou, China). Titanium oxysulfate (TiOSO4), ethanol (C2H5OH), cephalexin (C16H17N3O4S) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Urea (CO(NH2)2), citric acid (C6H8O7), methanol (CH3OH), isopropanol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO), and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). NaN3 was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corporation (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical or higher grade and used as received.

The TiO2/WO3/CQDs (TWC) was synthesized via a modified hydrothermal-calcining process with WO3 as support (as shown in Scheme 1 ). In a typical hydrothermal procedure for CQDs preparation, CO(NH2)2 (1.00 g) and citric acid (3.20 g) were dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water and mixed for 10 min, then the mixture was transferred to Teflon-inner stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling, the brown-yellow solution was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm (6 932 × g) for 30 min to remove large particles. Half of the supernatant was withdrawn and dried at 105 °C to obtain the CQDs.

The loading of TiO2 on WO3 was achieved by a hydrothermal method. Briefly, TiOSO4 (0.72 g) and CO(NH2)2 (0.48 g) were added to 16 mL of distilled water and magnetically stirred for 5 min, then the suspension was ultrasonicated for 30 min to reach complete dissolution of TiOSO4. A certain amount of WO3 powder was added into the solution under magnetic stirring, the mixture was then transferred into Teflon-inner stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 24 h. The formed TiO2/WO3 was washed by distilled water and ethanol, and dried at 80 °C. A series of TiO2/WO3 composites with different mass contents of WO3 (1%, 2%, 5%) was synthesized and denoted as TWx (TW1, TW2, TW5). For comparison, pure TiO2 was prepared with identical procedure but without WO3.

At the optimum WO3 to TiO2 ratio (determined as 2%), TiO2/WO3/CQDs composite was synthesized through a facile calcination method. In a typical procedure, 1.00 g of TiO2/WO3 powder and predetermined amounts of CQDs were co-dispersed in 60 mL ethanol and sonicated for 1 h. The ethanol was then evaporated under magnetic agitation at 75 °C and finally dried at 105 °C for 2 h. The obtained powder was grinded and calcined at 300 °C for 4 h. To obtain the optimal photocatalyst, a series of TiO2/WO3/CQDs composites with different mass contents of CQDs (1%, 3%, 5%) was synthesized and denoted as TWCx (TWC1, TWC3, TWC5).
The detailed material characterizations methods are presented in Text S1 in the Supporting Information (SI).

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared materials was evaluated by degradation of cephalexin under simulated solar irradiation from a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with an AM 1.5G filter. The temperature was controlled by circulating cooling water around 298 ± 2 K, and the light intensity was measured as 95 mW·cm−2 (Perfect Light PL-MW2000 Photoradiometer). Prior to photocatalytic degradation, 0.125 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in a photo-reactor with 250 mL of cephalexin (10 mg·L−1) solution, then the pH of suspension was adjusted to 7.0 and kept stirring for 30 min to ensure the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The photocatalytic degradation was initiated by exposing the photo-reactor to simulated solar light, and 2 mL of suspension was withdrawn and filtered (0.22 μm nylon syringe filter) at predetermined time intervals for cephalexin analysis. To evaluate the visible light activity of the materials, experiments were also conducted with a UV cut-off filter to remove UV irradiation (≤420 nm).

The contributions of various reactive species were indirectly investigated via radicals trapping experiments. Typically, four different scavengers, including ammonium oxalate (AO) at 700 mg·L−1, isopropanol (IPA) at 1200 mg·L−1, NaN3 at 200 mg·L−1, and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) at 80 mg·L−1 were selected to quench photo-generated holes (h +), hydroxyl radical (OH), singlet oxygen (1O2) and OH, and superoxide radical (O2 –), respectively [16]. In addition, the presence of OH and O2 – radicals were also captured in deionized water and methanol, respectively, by using the trapping agent 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrolin-N-oxide (Dimethyl Pyroline Oxide, DMPO) and recorded by electronic paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique.

The cephalexin concentration was determined by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, LC-20AT, Shimadzu, Japan) coupled with an Inertsil ODS-Sp column (5 μm, 4.6 × 250 mm), the photodiode array detector (PDA) at detection wavelength of 260 nm offered a detection limit of 0.1 mg·L−1. The degradation intermediates were analyzed by LC-MS system (Q Exactive plus LCMS-8050, Thermo Fisher, USA). The mineralization efficiency was assessed via the total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (TOC-VCPN, Shimadzu, Japan). The DFT calculation was conducted to help interpreting the photocatalytic degradation mechanism and pathways (Text S2 of the SI). The ecotoxicity of cephalexin and its degradation intermediates were evaluated by quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) using toxicity assessment software (T.E.S.T.) [21].

The stability and reusability of TiO2/WO3/CQDs were evaluated by conducting five consecutive reusing experiments. After each experiment, the spent photocatalyst was collected using 0.22 μm filter membrane and dried for next reusing cycle. The used photocatalyst was collected for material characterization, and the aqueous phase of the suspension was also collected to analyze the leaching of tungsten.

The microstructures of commercial WO3, synthesized TiO2 and TiO2/WO3/CQDs (prepared under optimum condition) were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images (Fig. 1 ) reveal that WO3, and TiO2/WO3/CQDs are flake-shaped and irregular spherical, respectively. The TiO2 synthesized by hydrothermal treatment was observed as agglomerated flower-shaped particles with average size of 45 nm (Fig. 1b), the clear lattice fringe of TiO2 with lattice spacing of 0.35 nm indicates the presence of anatase with the (101) crystal plane. The lattice distance of WO3 is 0.36 nm, which corresponds to the monoclinic phase plane of (020) (Fig. 1d), and it is further confirmed by XRD analysis. Fig. 1e and f clearly show that the CQDs with the particle size distribution of 6–14 nm are evenly distributed on the materials. The CQDs with lattice spacing of 0.327 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [22]. The anatase type of TiO2 offers relatively high photocatalytic activity for the material, and the sheet-like WO3 can provide a large number of active sites. These are beneficial to form the stable heterojunctions with TiO2 and CQDs. In the photocatalytic process, the uniformly distribution of CQDs and well heterojunction of TiO2/WO3 are beneficial to the separation and transfer of carriers, thereby retarding the electron-hole recombination and improving photocatalytic activity [16].

The X-Ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of TiO2, WO3, and TiO2 loaded with different amounts of WO3 and CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 a. The diffraction peaks of pure WO3 at 23.06°, 23.56°, 24.30°, 26.56°, 28.84°, 33.24°, 33.60°, 34.12°, 46.62°, 49.90°, and 55.86° are corresponding to the crystal planes of (002), (020), (200), (102), (120), (022), (202), (220), (112), (140), and (420), respectively, according to JCPDS data (No. 83–0950) [23]. The diffraction peaks of TiO2 at 25.24°, 37.78°, 48.02°, 53.92°, 55.02°, 62.66°, 70.22°, and 75.14° are attributed to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (220), and (215) orientations of anatase structure, respectively, according to JCPDS data (No. 21–1272) [24]. Evidently, no other impurities of TiO2 or WO3 were detected by XRD analysis. For the synthesized heterojunctions with WO3 and CQDs, the clear anatase and WO3 monoclinic phase diffraction peaks were observed, conforming the combination of WO3 with TiO2, and which indicates that the material owns relative high UV activity base on the large anatase percentage. However, there is no significant characteristic peaks of CQDs for TiO2/WO3/CQDs, which is possibly due to the low loading ratio of CQDs (3%) on the composite [25]. Some other studies have shown that even if the loading of CQDs reaches 8%, there is still no significant XRD diffraction peaks present [26].

The N2 adsorption–desorption analysis reveals that the TiO2, TW2 and TWC3 are all typical IV adsorption curves with H3 hysteresis loops (as shown in Fig. 2b). The pore size distribution of TWC3 is predominantly around 10 nm, revealing the mesoporous structure of the material. When the mesopores are capillary condensed and filled during the adsorption of contaminants, the macropores can continue to adsorb and even form a multi-molecular layer. There is no hysteresis ring for the N2 adsorption–desorption of WO3 due to its lamellar structure. The hydrothermal synthesized TiO2 has large specific surface area (103.4 m2·g−1, as shown in Table S1), while the incorporation of WO3 increased the pore size and pore volume of the material. Although the loading of CQDs slightly reduced the pore volume of the composite, it increased the specific surface area of the material thus provided more surface-active sites, which is conducive to the degradation of pollutants.

The photocatalytic activity of WO3 and synthesized materials, including TiO2, TWx with different Ti/W ratio and TWCx with different CQDs dosage, were studied through conducting the photocatalytic degradation of cephalexin under simulated solar light (as shown in Fig. 4 ). The removal of cephalexin through adsorption by all the materials were <5% in dark. The cephalexin can be hardly degraded in the absence of catalyst (Fig. 4a), indicating that cephalexin has high stability and long presence in water environment. The photocatalytic degradation of cephalexin with various photocatalysts can be well described by the first-order reaction kinetics, and the fitted rate constants are shown in Table S2.

The photocatalytic degradation rate of cephalexin by various materials follows the order of WO3<TiO2<TWx< TWCx. WO3 has narrow band gap and is capable of utilizing part of the visible light to degrade cephalexin, thus achieves much faster cephalexin degradation. However, the fast recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs still limits its photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The TiO2, even with low visible light absorbance, still showed much higher photocatalytic activity than WO3. When the coupling of WO3 with TiO2 was investigated (Fig. 4c and Table S2), it is evident that the loadings of WO3 from 0 to 1 and 2 wt% dramatically increased the k obs from 0.0149 ± 0.0005 to 0.0211 ± 0.0001 and 0.0254 ± 0.0008 min−1, respectively. However, a higher WO3 percentage decreased the k obs to 0.022 ± 0.0007 min−1. The coupling of WO3 with TiO2 affects the photocatalytic activity of the materials based on the following mechanisms: (1) The loading of WO3 can reduce the band gap of the composite, which broaden the visible light utilization spectrum, thus improving the photocatalytic activity of the materials under sunlight (It is further confirmed by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) in Fig. 7). (2) The heterojunction of WO3 and TiO2 is favorable for the transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs due to the formed internal electric field [16], the extended life of charge carrier and retarded recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs make the composite more reactive [29]. However, excessive amorphous WO3 layer on the material obstructs the light absorption of TiO2 and reduces photocatalytic activity [16,24]. Therefore, the optimal WO3 percentage is 2%, and TW2 with highest photocatalytic activity was used for subsequent tests.

CQDs with different mass ratios were loaded on the optimized material (TW2), and their photocatalytic activity is shown in Fig. 4b. Increasing the CQDs loadings from 0 to 1 and 3 wt% increased k obs from 0.0254 ± 0.0008 to 0.0331 ± 0.0004 and 0.0460 ± 0.0014 min−1, respectively. However, further increasing the CQDs dosage to 5% reduced the k obs to 0.037 ± 0.0032 min−1. Specifically, the degradation efficiency of cephalexin by TWC1, TWC3 and TWC5 within 60 min are 86.3 ± 2.0%, 94.0 ± 2.1% and 81.7 ± 2.4%, respectively (as shown in Fig. 4d). Acting as active sites, CQDs can not only enhance the absorption of visible light but also improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs of TWC3. However, excessive loading of CQDs tend to occupy surface active sites of the TiO2/WO3 and inhibit the O2 adsorption [30], which makes the TWC5 less effective than TWC3. Therefore, TiO2/WO3/CQDs (TWC3) with the WO3 loading of 2% and CQDs percentage of 3% was used as the optimized product, which demonstrated 3.1- and 46.6-times photocatalytic activity of pure TiO2 and WO3, respectively. Azimi and Nezamzadeh-Ejhieh [31] supported PbS-CdS onto clinoptilolite as photocatalysts for the degradation of cephalexin. The materials achieved ~ 76% of cephalexin degradation at 120 min, whose photocatalytic activity is ~ 30% lower than the materials synthesized in this work. Another work applied the functionalized N,S-doped CQDs embedded ZnO nanoflower for photocatalytic degradation of cephalexin. The removal efficiency reached ~87% in 50 min, which is close to our study; however, the intensity of Xe lamp used in that work is about three times of the lamp in our study [32]. Therefore, compared with other works, the synthesized TWC3 exhibits high photocatalytic activity.

The photo-generated free radicals (e.g., OH, O2 −, and 1O2) and holes are mainly contributed to the photocatalytic degradation of organics under sunlight. Therefore, the contributions of free radicals in the photocatalysis process were further investigated through radical quenching experiments [33]. Fig. 5 a shows that the addition of ammonium oxalate (AO, as hole scavenger) only slightly decreased the degradation rate of cephalexin, demonstrating that the photogenerated holes participated in the cephalexin degradation but with less important contribution. The presence of isopropanol (IPA, as OH scavenger) reduced the kobs value by 59.6% (shown in Table S3 and Fig. 5b), indicating that OH played an important role in the photocatalytic degradation process. The addition of NaN3 (a scavenger for both OH and 1O2) decreased the kobs by 89.0%. Therefore, the 1O2 is a more important radical for the cephalexin degradation when compared with OH. Moreover, the addition of 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ, as O2 – scavenger) dramatically inhibited the degradation rate (approximate 90.5%), which reveals that O2 – played the most important role. Therefore, the contribution of radicals in the photocatalytic systems follows the order of: O2 –> 1O2> OH. Many studies revealed that O2 – can efficiently decompose organic substances, and it can also convert to other active substances (i.e., OH and H2O2) to degrade organic substances indirectly [9]. The EPR technology, using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrolin-N-oxide (DMPO) as trapping agent, was applied to further confirm the generation of OH and O2 – radicals [34]. Six DMPO-O2 – characteristic signal peaks can be clearly observed under light as shown in Fig. 5c and d. The intensity of the four DMPO-OH characteristic peaks is relative weak, but almost no characteristic peaks can be observed in the dark. Therefore, the EPR results directly proved the generation of abundant O2 – and a small amount of OH radicals in the TWC3 based photocatalysis system, which is in good accordance with the radical quenching experiments.

To further demonstrate the mechanism of promoted photocatalytic activity of the WO3 and CQDs loaded TiO2, photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the materials were obtained, at the excitation at 340 nm, to reveal the charge carrier separation efficiency (as shown in Fig. 6
a). The TiO2 and WO3 exhibited typical PL peaks centered at 470 nm, which can be due to the recombination of the conduction band (CB) electron and trapped hole or electron quenching. The WO3 loaded TiO2 demonstrated lower PL intensity, indicating that the WO3 played a key role in promoting the photogenerated electron-hole separation [16]. The emission peak of TWC2 decreased dramatically and shifted slightly to shorter wavelength, which can be due to the decreased charge carrier recombination derived from the attached CQDs on the TW2 surface. The charge separation properties of the materials are consistent with their photocatalytic activities for the cephalexin degradation.

In addition, the charge transfer resistance of the photocatalysts were investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and shown in the form of a Nyquist plot (Fig. 6b). The largest arc curve radius of WO3 indicated that WO3 had high charge separation resistance. Nevertheless, the loading of WO3 on TiO2 can form a heterojunction which lowered the charge separation resistance, thus the TW2 showed lower charge separation resistance than TiO2 [35]. The small arc curve radius of TWC3 reveals that the incorporation of CQDs can further reduce the charge separation resistance, indicating that effective electron-hole separation and faster electron transfer occurred on TWC3.

Photocurrent measurement was carried out to further evaluate the recombination of photogenerated charge carrier of the as-prepared catalysts. Fig. 6c shows the transient photocurrent response of pure TiO2, WO3, synthesized TW2 and TWC3 electrodes under intermittent light irradiation at 20 s intervals. Under simulated solar irradiation, the photocurrent densities of WO3 and TiO2 are 0.03 and 0.20 μA cm−2, respectively. Their relatively low photocurrent densities can be attributed to the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. It is worth noting that the photocurrent response of TWC3 is 2 times and 14 times that of TiO2 and WO3, respectively. This can be due to that (1) the formation of heterojunction between TiO2 and WO3 is conducive to the separation of electron-hole pairs; (2) CQDs, as excellent electron acceptors, can attract photo-generated electrons to the surface of TW3, which are beneficial for inhibiting electron-hole recombination; (3) electrons can be excited from CQDs to the CB of TiO2, further increasing the electron-hole separation [36,37].

To reveal the promoted visible light activity of the materials, the optical properties of the prepared photocatalyst were characterized by UV–Vis DRS (as shown in Fig. 7a and b). Particularly, the absorption edges of TiO2, WO3, TW2, and TWC3 are 390 nm, 475 nm, 450 nm and 475 nm, respectively, which indicate that the loading of WO3 and CQDs significantly extended the light absorption. In order to quantitatively demonstrate the band gap change of the WO3 and CQDs modified TiO2, the tangent intercept of the function (F(R) × hv)1/2 to the photon energy hv is plotted in Fig. 7b according to direct semiconductor property of WO3 [38]. Kubelka–Munk function F(R) and photon energy hv can be calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2):

(2)
hv =
1240 λ
where R is the reflectivity; λ is the wavelength (nm).

The calculated band gap widths of TiO2, WO3, TW2 and TWC3 are 3.18, 2.61, 2.76 and 2.61 eV, respectively (as shown in Fig. 7b). Therefore, the introduction of WO3 and CQDs significantly decreased the band gap of TiO2, which extended the light adsorption to longer wavelength and altered the redox ability of the composite. It is known that the introduction of CQDs can hardly change the valence band (VB) of TW2 but make its CB more positive, and the presence of CQDs is more likely to provide a lower empty orbit for the VB [39]. The narrowing of the band gap enables the utilization of low-energy visible light and stimulating electron transition. However, the redox capacity will be weakened at the same time [40,41]. More importantly, the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes was inhibited due to the formation of heterojunction between TiO2, WO3 and CQDs. Overall, the material characterizations demonstrated that the promoted visible light activity, increased excitation, and suppressed recombination of electron-hole pairs are beneficial to the photocatalytic activity of the materials.

In order to further verify the red shift of the absorption edge of the new material and its positive effect on the photocatalytic process, a UV420 filter was used to remove UV portion (λ<420 nm) of the simulated solar light, and compare the improved photocatalytic activity of the new material under both visible and UV light. Under visible light, the kobs for the cephalexin degradation are 0.00034 ± 0.00002 and 0.00031 ± 0.00002 min−1 by WO3 and TiO2, respectively, which indicate that the pure materials have low visible light activity. It worth noting that the kobs is 0.0015 ± 0.0002 min−1 for TWC3, revealing that the incorporation of WO3 and CQDs on TiO2 has greatly promoted the visible light activity of the material (as shown in Fig. 7c). This result is in accordance with the UV Vis-DRS data: WO3 and CQDs supported on TiO2 has greatly improved the utilization of visible light and thus improved the photocatalytic performance. It is known that the CQDs loading on the TWC3 plays dual important roles for the improved photocatalytic activity. Firstly, the CQDs can serve as electron reservoir to trap the photogenerated electrons under light wavelength <450 nm (the adsorption edges of TW2), which facilitates the charge separation and enhances the photocatalytic activity. Secondly, the π conjugated CQDs can act as a photosensitizer, which makes the TWC3 to be reactive under longer wavelength. The CQDs can donate electrons to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2, leading to higher photocatalytic activity [42,43]. However, when the kobs of cephalexin degradation under UV and visible light are compared (Fig. 7d), the contribution of visible light is still relatively low. Therefore, the promoted photocatalytic by improved charge carrier separation can be superior to the enhanced visible light activity for this material.

The valance band (VB) positions of WO3 and TiO2 at the point of zero charge can be calculated by Equation (3) based on the total densities of the VB state spectra [44]:
(3)
E
VB
= X -
E e
+ 0.5 E g
(4)
E
CB
=
E
VB
- E g
where X is the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor, defined as the geometric mean of the absolute electronegativity of the constituent atoms; Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV), EVB is the VB edge potential and Eg is the band gap of the semiconductor. Then, the CB position can be obtained from Eq. (4), and the calculated band position of TiO2 and WO3 are summarized in Table S4.

The calculated EVB of WO3 and TiO2 are 3.40 and 2.68 eV (vs Normal Hydrogen Electrode (NHE)), respectively, the corresponding ECB are 0.79 and −0.50 eV. Therefore, the TW2 is a typical type II heterojunction according to the staggered band levels of WO3 and TiO2. The photo-generated holes in the VB of WO3 tend to transfer to the TiO2 surface under internal electric field, and the electrons in the CB of TiO2 tend to transfer to the WO3 surface (Eqs. 5–6) [45]. The key mechanism for the promoted photocatalytic activity of TWC3 is proposed in Fig. 8 . Other than the mechanism of TiO2/WO3 heterojunction, the electrons can be excited from CQDs to the CB of the TiO2 to improve the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the CQDs can serve as electron reservoir to trap the photogenerated electrons under wavelength <450 nm (the adsorption edges of TW2), which further facilitates the charge separation. The above photocurrent, PL, and EIS studies have demonstrated that both the TiO2/WO3 heterojunction and incorporation of CQDs have efficiently separated the photoexcited electrons and holes, and transferred the charge carriers faster than individual semiconductors (as shown in Fig. 7). From the aspect of promoted visible light activity, the loading of WO3 (with lower band gap) could significantly extend the light absorption to longer wavelength. Moreover, the π conjugated CQDs can act as photosensitizer to excite and donate electrons at longer wavelength, leading to higher visible light activity [43]. However, when the kobs of cephalexin degradation under UV and visible light are compared (Fig. 7d), the promoted photocatalytic activity by improved charge carrier separation is superior to the enhanced visible light activity.

(6) WO3 + hv → WO3 (eW

(7) cephalexin + h+ → cephalexin + → → CO2 + H2O

(9) OH– (surface) + h+ → OH
(10) O2 + e − → O2 –
(11) O2 + 2H+ + 2e − → H2O2

(13)

(14) 2O2 – + 2H+ → 1O2 + H2O2
(15) cephalexin + ROS → products

The photogenerated holes on VB of WO3 has relative high oxidation potential, which can directly attack pollutants or generate OH groups to indirectly degrade organics (Eqs. 7–9). However, the oxidation potential of the holes is decreased after migrating to the VB of TiO2, which makes the formation of OH from OH– less likely to occur [46]. The CB potential of TiO2 is lower than that of
E
O 2
/ ·
O 2 -
(−0.33 eV vs NHE), thus the electrons on CB of TiO2 can react with the surface sorbed O2 to produce O2 – [47]. The electrons on CB of WO3 has higher redox potential and the formation of H2O2 are likely to occur. The cephalexin has strong light absorption at the UV region due to its benzene ring and large number of electron-rich double bonds. The solar irradiation can excite cephalexin to activated state and undergo photosensitized photodegradation. However, the photoactivated cephalexin can be quenched by O2 and other quenchers presented in aqueous phase [48], which eliminate the photodegradation process of cephalexin (as shown in Fig. 4a). In this study, the addition of CQDs decreased the CB and narrowed the band gap of the TWC3, which makes the injection and transfer of photo-excited HOMO electrons of cephalexin to the CB of TWC3 possible. Therefore, the lower orbits of CQDs can act as intermediate energy levels between HOMO of cephalexin and CB of TW2, and providing excellent electron transfer channels [39]. The electrons excited by cephalexin LUMO will be transferred to TiO2 or even WO3 under the internal electric field. In addition, the photosensitization effect of CQDs are partially contributed to the improved photocatalytic activity via improving the visible light utilization efficiency [49]. In accordance with the radical quenching and ESR results, the photogenerated electrons and holes will finally form a series of active substances (e.g., OH, O2 –, H2O2 and 1O2) and involve in the degradation of cephalexin (Equations 10–15).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density functional theory calculation and photocatalytic degradation pathway
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation intermediates of cephalexin were identified using LC/MS (as shown in Fig. S2). Combining with the calculated Fukui index and Hammett constant of cephalexin molecule, the possible degradation pathway of cephalexin is proposed in Fig. 9 . As shown in Fig. 9b, the active site with larger f 0 value is highlighted with darker red color, and which is more vulnerable to be attacked by free radicals [50]. Evidently, the f 0 of O18, N19 and C31 (which circled in the red in Fig. 9a) are 0.1355, 0.1763 and 0.0902, respectively, indicating that these three positions are more likely to be attacked during the photocatalysis process. Hammett's constant is frequently used to characterize the influence of substituents on the electron distribution characteristics of molecules, and is often used to quantitatively describe the reactivity of organic substances toward oxidation [51]. The positive Hammett's constant value represents an electron-withdrawing group, and the positive potential region is usually centered on the H atom of –OH (the red region in the Fig. 9c); on the contrary, the negative Hammett's constant value represents an electron-donating group, where the negative potential region is usually centered on O atoms (the blue region in the Fig. 9c). The region with higher negative potential will preferentially be attacked by free radicals. From Fig. 9b, the C-N with a lower bonding energy in the nitrogen-containing four-membered ring and the C-S bond in the S-containing six-membered ring in the cephalexin molecule are most likely to be attacked. N19 in the cephalexin molecule has the highest f 0, so the C-N bond was likely to be broken by free radicals first. It makes the C14 atom connected with the O18 atom with the second highest f 0 to be further oxidized. At the same time, the C31 atom with the third highest Fukui index (f 0 = 0.0902) is also very vulnerable to free radical, resulting in the broken of CC double bond and formation of carboxyl group. This type of reactions have been well documented in the photocatalytic degradation of cephalexin by other researchers [52]. Thereafter, these intermediates will be further oxidized to smaller molecules such as urea, acetone, and oxalic acid, acetic acid, propionic acid and toluene. Eventually, most organic matter is mineralized into H2O and CO2. Li et al. [53] found a similar cephalexin degradation pathway when the g-C3N4@ZnO was used under simulated solar irradiation. However, the intermediates C and G were not observed in their study, which may be due to the low intermediates concentration. The TOC analysis was conducted to further reveal the mineralization degree of cephalexin. Fig. 9e shows that cephalexin could be completely removed after reacting for 2 h, but 38.8% of TOC was still remained in the solution. 92.4% of TOC removal can be achieved after reacting for 4 h. As the reaction proceeds, more carboxyl-containing intermediates are formed, so the decrease in the solution pH can result in the slight decrease in inorganic carbon (IC) (as shown in Fig. 9e).

Based on the quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) prediction, the toxicity evaluation software (T.E.S.T.) was used to evaluate the acute toxicity (LC50, concentration of chemicals that cause 50% death of fathead minnow after 96 h), oral toxicity (LD50, the dose of a chemical agent that causes 50% rat death after oral administration), developmental toxicity and bioaccumulation factors of cephalexin and its degradation intermediates. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the LC50 of cephalexin is 0.71 mg·L−1, which can be classified as “highly toxic”. The hydroxylated products are far less toxic than cephalexin, and the products D, E, F, and G are “not harmful”. The oral toxicity (for rats) of most products, except product G, are higher than cephalexin. For instance, the product B, C, D, E, and F showed 1.64, 3.52, 1.87, 1.66, and 1.47 times toxic when compared with cephalexin. Similar results are found for the development toxicity and bioaccumulation factors, where most intermediates exhibited higher developmental toxicity than cephalexin, thus they are more likely to accumulate in animals. The mutagenicity of the Salmonella typhimurium strain was also evaluated by inducing the colony to return to growth (as shown in Table S5). It was found that the mutagenicity of most products was comparable to cephalexin, but the mutagenicity of product G was 17.5 times of cephalexin. In general, except for acute toxicity, the photocatalytic degradation intermediates showed higher toxicity than cephalexin. Therefore, it is necessary to ensure the high mineralization efficiency of the cephalexin and intermediates to guarantee the water safety. According to the TOC measurement and toxicity analysis, the cephalexin was rapidly converted into intermediates in the first 1 h, and the toxicity was slowly decreased. Thereafter, the intermediates were gradually mineralized, and the toxicity in the solution was rapidly decreased. The mineralization efficiency of photocatalysis system can reach 92.4% after reacting for 4 h, and further extending of reaction time can completely eliminate the potential biological risks of cephalexin in wastewater.

Stability is an important parameter for the cost-effective application of photocatalysts. Herein, the photocatalytic stability of TWC3 was tested by the recycling experiments. After each experiment, the suspended catalyst was membrane filtered and dried at 60 ℃ for the next cycle. Fig. 11 a shows that TWC3 still exhibited high photocatalytic activity after five reusing cycles. The XRD pattern of the used material shows no significant difference with the unused material (as shown in Fig. 11b). Moreover, no leached tungsten was detected (<0.05 mg/L) by the inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) analysis, indicating the high physicochemical stability of the TWC3. Therefore, both the reusing experiments and material characterizations demonstrated that the TWC3 is relatively stable, and owns high potential for long-term environmental application. However, it should be noted that the engineering application of nanomaterials for water treatment still need to overcome many challenges, such as the separation of nanomaterials from water phase, complex synthetic process and high costs. The future work will focus on material optimization, including loading material on supporters to facilitate its separation, using low-cost precursor, and optimizing synthesis method.

A ternary heterojunction (TiO2/WO3/CQDs, TWC3) with fast charge carrier separation and improved visible light activity was prepared by a facile hydrothermal-calcining method. The photocatalytic activity of prepared TWC3 was 3.1- and 46.6-times that of TiO2 and WO3 for cephalexin degradation under simulated solar irradiation. Material characterizations reveal that TWC3 is composed of sheet-like WO3 skeletal structure with evenly loaded TiO2 and CQDs, and with large specific surface area. The formation of TiO2/WO3 heterojunction narrowed the band gap and facilitated the photogenerated electrons and holes separation. The loading of CQDs, as an excellent photo-generated electron channel, further improved the charge carrier separation. Moreover, the π conjugated CQDs can act as photosensitizer to extend the light response of TWC3 to longer wavelength. Experiments showed that the promoted photocatalytic activity predominantly attributes to the improved charge carrier separation, while the enhanced visible light activity plays a less important role. Several reactive species were identified in the photocatalysis system, and their contributions to cephalexin degradation follows the order of: O2 –>1O2>OH>h+ . Based on the degradation intermediates analysis and DFT calculation, the photocatalytic degradation pathway of cephalexin was proposed. The ecotoxicity of some degradation intermediates was found higher than that of cephalexin, thus extended photocatalytic reaction time (>4h) are required to ensure sufficient mineralization. Overall, the TiO2/WO3/CQDs has the potential to be used for removing emerging contaminants in water environment.
